planting.note: Direct sow in spring in lean, well-drained, sandy or loamy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; chalky bluestem is an excellent Gulf Coastal Plain grass for sunny, dry to moderately moist sites in zones 7–10.

directions: Plant in lean, well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; chalky bluestem is a native grass of the Atlantic and Gulf Coastal Plain, closely related to big bluestem (A. gerardii) and broomsedge (A. virginicus) but distinct in the powdery, chalky-white bloom (waxy coating) that covers the stems and upper leaf sheaths, giving the plant a cool, glaucous appearance that sets it apart in the landscape. Like broomsedge, the foliage turns warm shades of bronze and copper in fall, providing excellent winter interest in the ornamental garden. The fine, fluffy seed heads in fall are attractive and provide seeds for numerous sparrows and other small birds. A reliable grass for dry, sunny spots in the Gulf South where it grows naturally in open pine flatwoods, old fields, and sandy roadsides. Combines beautifully with muhly grass, native wildflowers, and yuccas in low-maintenance Gulf Coast landscapes.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in dry, lean, sandy, slightly acidic soil in full sun; Florida bluestem is adapted to the droughty, nutrient-poor sandy soils of pine flatwoods and sandhills in the Florida peninsula and adjacent Gulf Coast states.

directions: Plant in dry to moderately moist, lean, sandy, slightly acidic soil in full sun; Florida bluestem is an endemic of Florida and the immediate Gulf Coast region, growing in dry, open pine flatwoods and longleaf pine sandhills where its deep root system accesses subsoil moisture during droughts. Taller than most bluestems, the plant produces attractive, arching, blue-green foliage in summer that transitions to warm copper and bronze tones in fall, creating a bold vertical element in the landscape. The fluffy, silvery-white seed heads wave attractively in the breeze and provide food for native sparrows and grassland birds. An outstanding native grass for dry, sandy, acidic soils of the Gulf South where it is ecologically appropriate and naturally sustainable. Excellent in restoration plantings of longleaf pine ecosystems and in low-maintenance landscapes on sandy soils where other grasses fail from drought.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in moist to dry, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade; variable panicgrass is a woodland species native to the understory of open deciduous and mixed forests, one of the few native grasses truly adapted to shaded conditions.

directions: Plant in moist to well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; variable panicgrass is one of the most important native Dichanthelium species of woodland and forest-edge habitats across eastern North America. The Dichanthelium grasses are a unique genus of native panicgrasses that form rosettes of broad basal leaves in fall and winter, bloom in spring, then produce a second wave of smaller flowers through summer — an unusual bimodal blooming pattern shared across the genus. This species occupies the woodland understory with particular success, tolerating a degree of shade that excludes most native grasses. The fine, loosely branched seed panicles are a critical, high-quality food source for woodland-edge sparrows, juncos, and other granivorous birds. Excellent in the shaded section of a rain garden, at woodland edges, and as a low-growing grass layer beneath canopy trees in native plant gardens.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in lean, well-drained, sandy or rocky, slightly acidic soil in full sun; rhizomatous little bluestem differs from common little bluestem (S. scoparium) by having shallow rhizomes that allow it to spread into a loose, spreading mat rather than a tight bunch — making it more effective as a groundcover grass.

directions: Plant in lean, well-drained, dry to moderately moist, sandy or rocky, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; rhizomatous little bluestem is a Gulf Coastal Plain endemic closely related to the widely cultivated little bluestem (S. scoparium) but with the important additional trait of short rhizomes that allow the plant to spread into a loose colony — a significant advantage for erosion control on sandy slopes and for naturalizing large dry areas with a weed-suppressing native grass groundcover. The blue-green summer foliage, silvery-white fall seed heads, and brilliant copper-bronze winter color mirror the ornamental qualities of common little bluestem, while the spreading habit provides better coverage of large areas. Native to dry pine flatwoods, sandhills, and sandy openings in the Gulf states from Florida to Texas. An outstanding native grass for large-scale sandy slope plantings and mass groundcover applications in the warm-season Gulf South garden.

planting.note: Direct sow or plant divisions in spring in moist to wet, slightly brackish to fresh, sandy or clay soil in full sun; big cordgrass grows naturally along tidal freshwater river margins, freshwater marshes, and the brackish upper zones of estuaries. It spreads by strong rhizomes to form dense colonies.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, slightly acidic to brackish, sandy to clay soil in full sun at pond margins, tidal freshwater marshes, and streambanks; big cordgrass is the largest of the native cordgrasses, producing bold, coarse, erect culms to 6–10 feet in height that create an impressive, wetland structural element. Growing naturally along tidal rivers, upper brackish marshes, and freshwater wetland edges from the Atlantic to the Gulf Coast, it tolerates occasional salinity but thrives equally well in fresh water. The large, comb-like seed heads in late summer and fall are distinctive and provide abundant seeds for marsh birds including secretive rails. The dense, vigorous rhizomes are highly effective for shoreline stabilization and marsh restoration along tidal rivers and freshwater margins. A bold, architectural statement plant for large pond edges, wetland gardens, and stream bank stabilization in zones 5–10.
